turns.content: I'm trying to implement a G-counter in my distributed system tutorial, but I'm struggling with the merge logic. My code uses a simple integer for each node's count, and when two nodes sync, they just add their counts together. Is this actually correct or do I need something more complex like an OR-set or LWW register approach?

Your current implementation is a classic mistake for CRDTs; you're treating it like a standard database transaction log instead of using the actual CRDT properties. A G-counter needs to track not just the value but also which node contributed that increment so the merge operation can handle duplicates correctly without double-counting. The crdts crate provides an OR-set implementation that handles this by tracking contributions rather than raw values, and it uses a specific trait for the counter logic. You should look at how the LWW register works to understand why simple addition fails in distributed environments.

I see your point about double counting, but I'm still confused about how the crdts crate actually implements this merge. It seems like it's just doing some arithmetic on the counts, which feels very similar to what I was trying to do before.

The key difference is that the crdts crate doesn't just store a final integer; it stores a collection of contributions where each contribution includes metadata about who made which change. When you call the merge function on two G-counters, the system iterates through every individual increment from both sides and aggregates them based on their unique identifiers rather than summing raw totals directly. This ensures that if Node A incremented twice and Node B incremented once, the result correctly reflects three total increments regardless of sync order.
That makes more sense now, but I'm still not sure how this relates to the OR-set concept you mentioned earlier. Are these fundamentally different approaches or just variations on the same theme?

The OR-set is a specific type of CRDT designed for sets where order doesn't matter, while a G-counter tracks signed integers with contributions. They share the underlying philosophy of using contributions and metadata to handle concurrent updates safely across nodes, but they serve different data types. The crdts crate implements both as separate traits because the merge logic differs based on whether you're dealing with ordered sequences or unordered sets.
Okay, so if I want to implement a G-counter myself without using the crdts crate, what's the minimal set of fields and operations I need? Do I just need a vector of contributions?

You'll need at least a vector of contributions where each entry stores the value, the node ID that made it, and a timestamp. The merge operation must iterate through both vectors, identify matching entries by node ID, and sum their values before storing them back into your local structure. You also need to implement the trait for serialization so you can send these contribution lists over the network instead of just sending raw integers.

Ah! So my previous attempt failed because I was only storing the final count, not tracking who made what change. That's why simple addition didn't work. But wait, shouldn't I be using something like a LWW register for this instead since it handles value comparison?

Not quite; the LWW register is designed for storing single values with timestamps to resolve conflicts based on who wrote last, whereas your G-counter needs to track multiple independent increments from different nodes. The LWW approach would break if you have conflicting updates because it only keeps one value per key. A proper G-counter implementation requires tracking all contributions rather than just the latest write.

I'm trying to implement a G-counter in Rust using the crdts crate, but I thought that since it's just an integer counter with signed values, I could just use std::sync::{Arc, Mutex} and wrap my u64 field inside Arc<Mutex<u64>>. That should handle all the distributed updates correctly if everyone syncs periodically.

That assumption is a common trap when moving from imperative concurrency to CRDT logic. While you can lock a single u64 with Arc<Mutex<u64>> for local consistency, it breaks the fundamental CRDT property where operations are applied without coordination or global state synchronization. The crdts crate specifically implements G-counter using its own internal representation that tracks both positive and negative increments to avoid the need for merging or locking entirely.

Exactly, I get it now, but you're saying we shouldn't even lock anything? That sounds too good to be true. If I don't use any locks on my local state, how do I prevent two nodes from updating the same counter at the same time and creating a race condition?

The crdts crate solves this by storing operations as immutable logs rather than mutable counters. When node A increments to +1, it sends an AddOp with value +1 to the cluster; when node B decrements to -2, it sends a SubOp with value -2. These are stored in the GCounter struct without any internal locking because each operation is idempotent and commutative by definition.
So I just send operations around? But what if two nodes try to increment at the exact same millisecond? Doesn't that mean we lose ordering or create a duplicate entry in the log?

You're right, but CRDTs don't rely on strict global ordering for correctness. The crdts crate ensures that even if two nodes apply +1 simultaneously, their operations will both be recorded and merged into the final value correctly upon synchronization. The key insight is that you never need to know who sent which operation first because the math guarantees convergence regardless of timing.
Okay so I can just implement this locally without worrying about network latency or ordering? That seems almost too simple compared to what I've seen in other distributed systems documentation.

It is simpler precisely because it avoids the complexity of consensus protocols like Raft or Paxos. The crdts crate handles the merge logic internally so your local GCounter struct only needs to track which operations have been applied. You don't implement any special synchronization code yourself; the library manages the convergence state.

I still feel like I'm missing something though. If everyone just applies operations independently, how does anyone actually know when they've received all the updates from other nodes to compute the final count?

The crdts crate provides a sync mechanism where each node periodically broadcasts its complete operation log. Once every node receives the full history of operations, it can re-apply them locally using the GCounter's merge logic to compute the exact same final integer value. This is why CRDTs are called conflict-free; they converge to identical states regardless of which node went first.

Okay, so I just need to make sure my local GCounter has access to the full operation log before computing the result. But what about other CRDT types like OR-sets or LWW registers? Do they follow similar patterns?

Yes, all CRDts in the crdts crate share this same philosophy but with different data structures. For example, an OR-set stores unique items without duplicates by tracking version numbers or timestamps per item rather than maintaining a global set state. Similarly, LWW registers rely on last-write-wins semantics where each operation carries metadata like timestamp or vector clock to resolve conflicts deterministically during merge.

I'm trying to implement a G-counter in the crdts crate but I keep thinking it's just a regular i32 wrapped with serde_json::Value. What is the actual difference between a standard integer and an OR-set when you are dealing with distributed updates?

Your assumption that a G-counter is merely a boxed integer is incorrect because it ignores the core requirement of CRDTs for conflict-free replication. An OR-set represents existence or membership without ordering, whereas a G-counter tracks signed increments and decrements across nodes using vector clocks to determine causality. The crdts crate handles this by implementing traits like Clone and Display on these specific types so you don't have to manage the underlying logic manually.
Okay, so I just need to implement Serialize for my custom counter? Why can't I just use the standard library's serialization traits?

Because implementing Serialize alone is insufficient; you must also respect the G-counter's invariant rules where increments and decrements must be causally ordered. The crdts crate provides a VectorClock struct that tracks logical time so your application can merge updates correctly without losing data consistency, which standard serialization does not provide.
That makes sense, but how do I actually use the LWW register to store these counters instead of just using a simple string key?

You should not use an LWW register for counters because LWW is designed for storing single values where only one version wins, whereas a G-counter requires tracking multiple concurrent updates to compute the final signed sum. Instead of using LWW, you must rely on the crdts crate's built-in GCounter type which automatically merges operations from different nodes without needing to manually implement an LWW strategy.

I'm back in the game after five years, so I remember that LWW register is basically like a simple version of a timestamp-based log where you just overwrite old values. But when I looked at the crdts crate docs for G-counter it seemed too complex compared to what I used to write with just an atomic counter.

That's exactly the common mistake beginners make, thinking CRDTs are just fancy versions of local variables. The crdts crate uses a G-counter which is actually a vector of integers where each entry represents a node in the cluster, and you can't really treat it like a single scalar unless you only have one replica.

Right, so I was trying to implement this by just reading all nodes from my database and summing them up, but that breaks consistency because the network hasn't fully converged yet. It feels like I'm assuming the data is already consistent before it actually is.

You are right to push back on that assumption. The crdts crate handles this by using a trait called ConsistentRead which ensures you only query after all updates have been propagated, so your local view never diverges from the global state until it's safe to proceed.
But how do I actually implement that check without building a full cluster manager myself? Does crdts crate provide a helper function for this?

Think of it like an OR-set but with counters. Imagine you have three friends passing notes in a shared notebook, and your G-counter is just tracking how many times each friend has written to the page. Before anyone can read the total count, they must wait until everyone who wrote has finished writing, similar to how you'd wait for all messages in an OR-set to arrive before aggregating them.
